text: Spanish The aim of this article is to assess EU policy with regard to the inclusion of essential elements clauses in EU trade agreements, which is the main way in which the trade-human rights nexus mandated by Article 205(2) TFEU is fulfilled. After outlining the structure of the clauses at issue, the article will examine the criticisms raised with respect to a series of issues: firstly, the fact that not all EU trade agreements contain essential elements clauses; secondly, the wording and scope of these clauses; and thirdly, the assessment and implementation of their monitoring and enforcement. Finally, the article will evaluate the effectiveness and legitimacy of the EU's policy regarding essential elements clauses, while analysing their impact on the credibility of the EU's normative external relations programme. English The EU policy of including essential elements clauses in its trade agreements is the main way of realizing the trade-human rights nexus mandated by Article 205(2) TFEU which requires the Union to use its external relations activity to promote its founding values. After analyzing the structure of the essential elements clauses, this paper examines the criticisms leveled in respect of the following issues: - (i) that not all of the EU's trade agreements include an essential elements clause; - (ii) that these clauses are not formulated in the same way, which raises questions as to their scope and effectiveness; - (iii) their implementation, that is the modalities of their monitoring and enforcement. The article ends with an assessment of the effectiveness and legitimacy of EU policy on essential elements clauses, with a particular focus on their impact on the credibility of the Union's normative external relations agenda.
